ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Rabri exists; mapped to the closest parent class 'milk-based dessert food product' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (milk-based dessert) used as the closest defensible category match; no specific Rabri entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Rabri or its immediate parent category in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Rabri is one small bowl of approximately 100g, which is the standard portion size served as a dessert or sweet accompaniment at Indian meals and celebrations.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Rabri values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using the closest published entry for concentrated sweetened full-cream milk dessert. Micronutrient values for potassium, calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, iron, zinc, selenium, and vitamins cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entry for 'Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D' and 'Sweetened condensed milk' as compositional anchors for the milk-reduction process.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Rabri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on the published GI of sweetened condensed milk and full-fat dairy desserts (GI range 40–55) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The high fat and protein content of the reduced milk matrix significantly attenuates glycemic response relative to the sugar content alone. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 18.0) / 100 = 9.36, rounded to 9.4 per 100g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat and caloric density from full-cream milk reduction
hypertension.guidance: Rabri is moderate in sodium (55 mg/100g) and rich in potassium (180 mg) and calcium (165 mg), which are beneficial for blood pressure regulation; however, its high saturated fat content (approximately 7.8 g/100g) and caloric density warrant portion restriction to no more than 50–100g for individuals with hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and concentrated carbohydrate from milk reduction
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rabri contains approximately 15.5 g of sugar per 100g from added sucrose and lactose concentration; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to small portions (≤50g) and preferably choose versions prepared with low-glycaemic sweeteners, monitoring postprandial glucose response carefully.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from full-cream dairy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content (approximately 7.8 g/100g) and estimated cholesterol (~38 mg/100g) from full-cream milk make Rabri a food to consume in moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, ideally limiting intake to occasional small servings as part of a diet otherwise low in saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar and full-fat dairy may be pro-inflammatory in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Rabri due to its refined sugar content, which may promote inflammatory pathways; portion control and substitution with no-calorie sweeteners are advisable.

lactose_intolerance.guidance: Rabri is prepared entirely from full-cream milk and is not suitable for individuals with lactose intolerance unless a lactose-free milk variant is used.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rabri (Hindi: रबड़ी) is a classic North Indian milk-based dessert with a rich, thick, and creamy consistency punctuated by soft, layered flakes of clotted cream known as 'lachhas'. It is prepared by slow-boiling full-cream milk in a wide, shallow iron pan (kadai) over a low flame for several hours. As the milk simmers, a thin skin of cream forms repeatedly on the surface; each layer is carefully skimmed and pushed to the sides of the pan. This process is repeated until the milk reduces to roughly one-third of its original volume, yielding a dense, yellow-hued concentrate. The accumulated cream layers are then folded back into the thickened milk, creating the characteristic flaky texture. The dessert is sweetened with sugar and perfumed with saffron strands and crushed green cardamom. Nutritionally, Rabri provides approximately 210 kcal per 100g, with 6.8g protein, 12.5g fat (predominantly saturated), and 18g carbohydrate including 15.5g sugar. It is a good source of calcium (165 mg), potassium (180 mg), riboflavin (0.35 mg), and vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg). Clinically, it is calorie-dense and high in saturated fat and added sugar, making it a food to consume in moderation for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or weight. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low), moderated by the high fat and protein matrix. Rabri is served warm or chilled and is a celebratory staple at festivals such as Holi and Diwali, weddings, and grand banquets. It is also widely used as a decadent topping for Jalebis, Malpuas, and Gulab Jamuns.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between Rabri and Kheer?

confidence_reason: Nutritional values for Rabri are adapted from ICMR-NIN published data for Indian milk-based desserts and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for whole milk and sweetened condensed milk as compositional anchors. No single exact Rabri entry exists in any approved primary source; values represent a well-supported adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on full-fat dairy desserts. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed as naturally zero — Rabri is a pure dairy and sugar preparation with no plant-fibre-containing ingredients. 2. Saturated fat (7.8g), monounsaturated fat (3.2g), polyunsaturated fat (0.4g), trans fat (0.2g), cholesterol (38mg), and water_g (58.0g) are estimated values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for whole milk (FDC ID 746782) and sweetened condensed milk (FDC ID 172187) scaled to the concentration ratio of Rabri preparation; these fields were not directly reported in the input record. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for full-fat dairy desserts; no direct Rabri GI measurement is available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 4. Portions in the input record contained four identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 100g. 5. Input provenance was 'ai_generated'; all values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central approved sources. 6. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact Rabri entry exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2024.
